questions.text: Which international instrument established the foundational principles for data protection in Europe prior to the GDPR?
options: A. Council of Europe Convention 108 (1981)
C. Treaty of Lisbon (2009)
B. European Convention on Human Rights (1950)
D. Schengen Agreement (1985)
questions.justification_correct: Convention 108 is the first legally binding international treaty on data protection, establishing core principles like lawfulness, purpose limitation, and data subject rights that influenced subsequent European privacy frameworks and the GDPR.
questions.justification_wrong: While ECHR protects privacy (Article 8), it is not specifically a data protection instrument. Lisbon Treaty and Schengen primarily address institutional/border matters, not data protection principles.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 3, which territorial scope principle applies to an organization based in the UK processing data of EU residents after Brexit?
options: D. UK organizations are exempt under post-Brexit adequacy decisions
A. Territorial scope applies only if the UK organization has an establishment in the EU
C. GDPR never applies to non-EU organizations regardless of data subject location
B. Offering goods or services to data subjects in the EU triggers GDPR applicability
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 3(2) establishes that non-EU organizations fall under GDPR when offering goods/services to or monitoring behavior of EU residents. Post-Brexit adequacy does not exempt organizations from applicability when targeting EU residents.
questions.justification_wrong: Article 3(1) covers establishments in the EU, but Article 3(2) extends applicability to non-EU entities. Adequacy decisions address data transfers, not applicability thresholds for targeting EU residents.

questions.text: What is the primary institutional role of the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under the GDPR?
options: A. To enforce GDPR violations directly against organizations in member states
C. To replace national Data Protection Authorities in all EU member states
B. To ensure consistent application of GDPR through guidelines, opinions, and dispute resolution
D. To prosecute criminal data protection offenses in European courts
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Chapter VII establishes the EDPB as a coordinating body ensuring consistent application through guidelines (Article 70), opinions, and binding decisions on disputes between DPAs (Article 65). It does not directly enforce or prosecute.
questions.justification_wrong: Enforcement remains with national DPAs (Articles 55-57). EDPB does not replace DPAs or prosecute crimes; criminal matters fall outside GDPR scope and are handled by member state authorities.

questions.text: The EU Charter of Fundamental Rights recognizes data protection as a distinct right separate from privacy. Under which articles are these rights enshrined?
options: B. Article 17 (privacy only, data protection is implied)
C. Articles 2 and 3 (dignity and integrity)
A. Articles 7 (privacy) and 8 (data protection)
D. Article 52 (scope of rights)
questions.justification_correct: The Charter (2000, revised 2007) explicitly recognizes privacy (Article 7) and data protection (Article 8) as separate, complementary fundamental rights, establishing the legal basis for GDPR as a fundamental rights instrument.
questions.justification_wrong: Article 17 addresses other rights. Data protection is not merely implied but independently enumerated in Article 8. Articles 52-54 address scope limitations, not substantive right definitions.

questions.text: How do national implementations of the GDPR through enabling legislation differ from direct applicability?
options: C. National laws replace the GDPR entirely with autonomous national standards
B. National laws specify procedures for DPA establishment and detail national exemptions where GDPR allows member state discretion
A. National laws create additional derogations and exemptions not permitted by GDPR Articles 6, 9, and 23
D. National laws are unnecessary because GDPR is directly applicable and requires no transposition
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Articles 6, 9, and 23 grant member states discretion for national exemptions (e.g., national security, public interest). National laws implement DPA frameworks and exercise these permitted flexibilities, complementing GDPR's direct applicability.
questions.justification_wrong: National laws cannot create unauthorized derogations (Option A) or replace GDPR (Option C). While GDPR is directly applicable, Option D overlooks necessary national implementing measures for DPA powers, procedures, and permitted exemptions.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the primary distinction between the GDPR's material scope and territorial scope?
options: B. Material scope applies only to public sector organisations; territorial scope applies exclusively to private companies
C. Material scope covers personal data processing; territorial scope is limited to EU Member States only
D. Material scope encompasses third-country transfers exclusively; territorial scope addresses intra-EU data flows
A. Material scope defines what data and activities are covered; territorial scope determines which organisations and jurisdictions fall under GDPR obligations
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Articles 2 and 3 establish material scope (personal data, processing) and territorial scope (establishment in EU, targeting EU residents). Material scope defines what is regulated; territorial scope determines applicability to controllers/processors regardless of location.
questions.justification_wrong: Incorrect interpretations confuse material with sectoral scope, misunderstand territorial application, or conflate specific provisions (transfers, establishment) with overall scope definitions under Articles 2-3.

questions.text: Under Council of Europe Convention 108, what is the fundamental principle that distinguishes it from earlier data protection frameworks?
questions.tag: History of European Privacy Law and Convention 108
options: A. It established the first binding international treaty recognising data protection as a human right with cross-border enforcement mechanisms
D. It limited personal data to identification data only and excluded special categories entirely
C. It created a supranational data protection authority with unilateral enforcement power
B. It exclusively addressed public sector data processing and excluded private sector organisations
questions.justification_correct: Convention 108 (1981) was groundbreaking as the first binding international instrument recognising data protection as a fundamental right. It established minimum standards applicable to both public and private sectors, enabling signatories to enforce obligations across borders.
questions.justification_wrong: Convention 108 applied to both sectors, created no supranational authority, and recognised broad data categories. It was supplemented by Protocol 108+ (2018) but fundamentally addressed human rights protection through binding treaty obligations.

questions.text: What is the legal status and primary function of the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under the GDPR?
options: A. An independent body composed of DPA representatives that provides coordinated enforcement guidance, issues guidelines, and promotes consistency across Member States
C. A judicial body with power to overturn national DPA decisions and impose binding fines on controllers
D. An advisory entity without formal decision-making authority, functioning solely to recommend policy changes
B. A European Commission directorate with direct investigative and enforcement authority over all GDPR violations
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 68 establishes the EDPB as an independent body where DPA leaders coordinate enforcement, issue consistent guidelines, and promote cooperation. Its decisions on consistency create binding guidance for national authorities, enhancing GDPR harmonisation.
questions.justification_wrong: EDPB lacks direct enforcement or investigative authority (DPAs retain these). It does not overturn decisions judicially or function merely as advisory. Its recommendations carry legal weight through consistency mechanisms and decision-making authority.

questions.text: How does the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights relate to GDPR enforcement and interpretation?
options: D. It establishes data protection as superior to all other rights, prohibiting any balancing with competing interests
B. It creates separate enforcement procedures that supersede GDPR complaint mechanisms at national DPAs
C. It applies exclusively to EU institutions and does not bind Member State implementation of GDPR
A. It provides constitutional protection for the right to personal data protection (Article 8) and privacy (Article 7), informing GDPR interpretation and limiting permissible processing restrictions
questions.justification_correct: Charter Articles 7-8 recognise privacy and data protection as fundamental rights. CJEU interprets GDPR through Charter principles, ensuring compliance with constitutional standards. GDPR implements Charter protections, and enforcement respects Charter-mandated rights balancing.
questions.justification_wrong: Charter binds Member State action implementing EU law; GDPR applies Charter standards. It does not create separate mechanisms or establish data protection as absolute. Rights require proportionate balancing under Article 52 Charter.

questions.text: What distinguishes the ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) from the GDPR in terms of scope and relationship?
options: C. The ePrivacy Directive applies only to public telecommunications providers and excludes private internet service operators
B. The ePrivacy Directive supersedes GDPR for all digital processing and establishes a separate enforcement regime independent of national DPAs

A. The ePrivacy Directive targets specific electronic communications sectors (telecommunications, internet) with detailed rules on cookies, marketing calls, and confidentiality of communications; it complements GDPR's horizontal framework
D. The ePrivacy Directive addresses exclusively consent mechanisms and overrides GDPR's lawfulness conditions where conflicts arise
questions.justification_correct: ePrivacy Directive (Article 1) targets electronic communications services with lex specialis rules: cookie consent, unsolicited marketing, confidentiality. It complements GDPR as sector-specific legislation. Proposed ePrivacy Regulation would modernise these rules while maintaining complementarity.
questions.justification_wrong: ePrivacy Directive does not supersede GDPR; both apply concurrently. It covers both public and private operators. GDPR provides general lawfulness; ePrivacy adds electronic communications specifics. Conflicts resolved through hierarchy principles (lex specialis).

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 4(11), which of the following best describes 'consent'?
options: A. Any freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous affirmation of will by the data subject
C. Implicit agreement demonstrated through continued use of services
B. A general agreement to processing without need for specificity regarding purposes
D. Written permission obtained once for all processing activities indefinitely
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 4(11) defines consent as freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous affirmation. Article 7 further requires clear affirmative action, prohibiting pre-ticked boxes or inactivity as valid consent mechanisms.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, and D violate GDPR requirements: B lacks specificity; C contradicts Recital 32 rejecting implicit consent; D violates Article 7(4) requiring consent to be specific per purpose.

questions.text: Which institution is responsible for monitoring GDPR compliance and has power to issue binding decisions against data processors across EU member states?
options: D. European Court of Justice (ECJ) exclusively
B. European Data Protection Board (EDPB) exclusively
A. National Data Protection Authorities (DPAs)
C. European Commission exclusively
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Articles 51-55 establish DPAs as independent authorities with investigative and corrective powers. EDPB (Article 68) coordinates via consistency mechanism but DPAs issue binding decisions. EDPB issues guidelines and handles consistency disputes.
questions.justification_wrong: B overstates EDPB role; C lacks direct enforcement powers under GDPR; D handles legal interpretation only. DPAs possess day-to-day enforcement authority per Article 55-57.

questions.text: The ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) applies to electronic communications. Which scenario falls within its material scope?
options: B. Processing health records in a hospital database for medical treatment
A. Sending unsolicited marketing calls to individuals' telephone numbers without prior consent
D. Collecting anonymized statistical data from website analytics
C. Storing encrypted data on an external server without any transmission
questions.justification_correct: Directive 2002/58/EC Article 21 prohibits unsolicited calls for direct marketing without prior consent. This is paradigmatic ePrivacy regulation governing electronic communications channels, distinct from data processing per se.
questions.justification_wrong: B governs health data under GDPR, not ePrivacy Directive; C involves no electronic communication transmission; D involves anonymized data outside Directive scope per Article 2(a).

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 6, which lawful basis requires explicit assessment before processing sensitive personal data under Article 9?
options: A. Legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f)) automatically satisfies Article 9 requirements
C. Contract performance (Article 6(1)(b)) exempts processors from Article 9 restrictions
D. Legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c)) overrides all Article 9 protections without limitation
B. Explicit consent (Article 6(1)(a)) and explicit consent under Article 9(2)(a) must be obtained separately
questions.justification_correct: GDPR requires dual basis: Article 6 provides lawful processing condition; Article 9 restricts special categories requiring separate legal basis. Article 9(2)(a) requires explicit consent distinct from standard Article 6(1)(a) consent requirements.
questions.justification_wrong: A ignores Article 9 restrictions; C and D incorrectly suggest Article 6 bases override Article 9 entirely, contradicting Articles 9(1)-(2) cumulative requirement structure.

questions.text: The Council of Europe Convention 108 (modernized 2018) differs from GDPR regarding territorial application. What is a key distinction?
options: D. Convention 108 supersedes GDPR for all signatory states
B. Convention 108 has no territorial limitations; GDPR restricts application to EU territory exclusively
A. Convention 108 applies only within Council of Europe member states; GDPR applies globally to EU/EEA operations
C. Both instruments apply identically across all non-EU countries without distinction
questions.justification_correct: Convention 108 (Protocol 2018) binds signatories within Council of Europe framework. GDPR Article 3 applies extraterritorially to non-EU controllers offering services to EU residents or monitoring behavior, significantly broader scope.
questions.justification_wrong: B inverts application; Convention 108 covers Council of Europe members. C ignores distinct frameworks. D incorrect—Convention 108 complements GDPR; signatory EU states apply both concurrently.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 6, which legal basis allows processing without explicit consent when a controller has a legitimate interest that outweighs data subject rights?
options: D. Vital interests (Article 6(1)(d))
A. Legitimate interest (Article 6(1)(f))
C. Compliance with legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c))
B. Performance of a contract (Article 6(1)(b))
questions.justification_correct: Article 6(1)(f) permits processing when necessary for legitimate interests pursued by the controller or third party, unless overridden by data subject rights. The balancing test requires demonstrating that the controller's interest outweighs fundamental rights.
questions.justification_wrong: Other bases require explicit consent, contractual relationship, legal obligation, or vital interest protection—none permit flexible balancing of competing interests as does Article 6(1)(f).

questions.text: Which supervisory authority has cross-border coordination responsibilities across EU member states under GDPR Article 56?
options: C. The national court of the member state
D. The European Commission exclusively
A. The lead supervisory authority (Article 56(1))
B. The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS)
questions.justification_correct: Article 56 establishes the lead supervisory authority mechanism where one DPA leads investigations in cross-border processing cases. Other DPAs provide cooperation and information per Article 61, enabling coordinated enforcement.
questions.justification_wrong: EDPS handles EU institution processing only; national courts lack investigative authority under GDPR; the Commission lacks direct supervisory enforcement power delegated to DPAs.

questions.text: Under the ePrivacy Directive 2002/58/EC, when must electronic marketing communications via email require prior explicit consent?
options: C. Only for unsolicited faxes and automated calls
D. Never—prior notification to DPA suffices
B. Only when sent to telephone numbers
A. When directed at consumers (B2C marketing) per Article 7
questions.justification_correct: Article 7 of the ePrivacy Directive mandates prior explicit consent for unsolicited commercial email to natural persons (consumers). Article 13 carves out B2B soft opt-in for existing customers in some jurisdictions.
questions.justification_wrong: Article 12 covers telephone/fax/automated calls; Article 13 permits B2B soft opt-in; DPA notification does not replace consent requirements; the Directive imposes substantive consent obligations.

questions.text: How does the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) under GDPR Article 68 differ from the Article 29 Working Party?
options: D. The two bodies operate in parallel with equal legal authority
B. EDPB replaced the Working Party with identical advisory-only functions
C. EDPB exists only in large member states; Working Party covered all EU
A. EDPB has binding decision-making authority on DPA disputes; Working Party was advisory only
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 68 establishes the EDPB as a legal body with binding authority to resolve DPA disputes (Article 65) and issue decisions. The Article 29 Working Party was a consultative body with no binding power.
questions.justification_wrong: They do not operate in parallel; EDPB is not limited by member state size; the Working Party was abolished upon GDPR entry into force and replaced with the independent EDPB.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 22, automated decision-making with legal effect is prohibited unless which condition applies?
options: A. The data subject has given explicit consent, or it is necessary for contract performance or authorized by law (Article 22(2))
D. The decision affects only non-sensitive data categories
B. The controller has conducted a prior data protection impact assessment (DPIA)
C. The data subject's personal data was collected with consent under Article 7
questions.justification_correct: Article 22(2) permits fully automated decisions with legal/similarly significant effect when: (a) necessary for contract performance, (b) authorized by EU/member state law, or (c) based on explicit consent. Safeguards and human review rights apply.
questions.justification_wrong: DPIAs are procedural safeguards, not exceptions; Article 7 consent alone does not authorize Article 22 processing; sensitivity of data does not override Article 22's core protection against automated decisions.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 4(11), what constitutes a 'legitimate interest' assessment when a controller processes personal data without explicit consent?
options: B. Automatic exemption if processing benefits the controller's business model
C. Unilateral determination by the controller without impact assessment
A. A three-part test: purpose necessity, balancing controller/data subject interests, and reasonable expectations
D. Compliance solely with contractual obligations
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interest requires: (1) purpose necessity, (2) balancing test weighing controller/data subject interests, (3) reasonable expectations. EDPB Guidelines 06/2014 confirm three-part assessment is mandatory for lawfulness.
questions.justification_wrong: Options B, C, D misinterpret legitimate interest: it is not automatic (B), requires external balancing (C), and extends beyond contracts (D). Recital 47 explicitly requires balancing and reasonableness assessment.

questions.text: Which supervisory authority holds primary enforcement responsibility under GDPR Article 55 when a non-EU controller processes personal data of EU residents without establishing a local representative?
options: C. The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) as primary authority
A. The DPA of the country where the controller is established
B. The DPA where the data subject is located or the processing substantially affects
D. A joint enforcement action by all EU DPAs simultaneously
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 3(2) establishes extraterritorial reach; Article 55 designates the DPA of the country where effects are felt. Article 4(23) defines 'establishment' broadly. EDPB Guidelines 3/2018 clarify jurisdiction based on targeting and effects, not controller location alone.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A ignores Article 3(2) extraterritorial scope. C confuses EDPS (supervises EU institutions) with DPA authority. D incorrectly assumes mandatory joint action; Article 55-60 allow one lead DPA under one-stop-shop mechanism.

questions.text: Under the ePrivacy Directive 2002/58/EC Article 13, what is the legal status of cookies and similar tracking technologies that do NOT require prior informed consent?
options: B. All cookies placed on user devices without distinction
C. Marketing and profiling cookies if the website displays a privacy notice
D. Third-party tracking pixels regardless of user interaction
A. Strictly necessary cookies for service delivery and security purposes
questions.justification_correct: ePrivacy Directive Article 13(1) exempts cookies strictly necessary for service delivery/security from prior consent requirement. EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 confirm 'strictly necessary' limited to authentication, load-balancing, fraud detection—not profiling or analytics.
questions.justification_wrong: B, C, D violate Article 13 exemption scope: Option B treats all cookies equally (incorrect), C assumes notice substitutes consent (false per ECJ Planet49), D misapplies necessity test. Only functionally necessary cookies are exempt.

questions.text: How does the GDPR Article 17 right to erasure ('right to be forgotten') interact with Article 6(1)(c) legal obligation processing?
options: C. Data subjects may demand erasure even if legal retention mandates apply
B. Erasure applies unless a conflicting legal obligation to retain exists; controller must document necessity
A. Legal obligations always override erasure rights; data must be retained indefinitely
D. Article 17 never applies to Article 6(1)(c) processing
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 17(3)(b) explicitly excludes erasure when legal obligations require retention. However, controller must demonstrate necessity and document conflicting legal basis. EDPB Opinion 05/2021 clarifies proportionality test still applies; retention beyond legal minimum is erasable.
questions.justification_wrong: Option A overstates legal privilege; retention must be proportionate and documented. C ignores Article 17(3) exceptions. D denies any application; Article 17 applies with documented exceptions only. Balancing principle (Recital 4) requires justified conflicts.

questions.text: Under Council of Europe Convention 108, what is the substantive legal relationship between its modernized 2018 Protocol and the GDPR's applicability to EU member states?
options: A. Convention 108 is superseded entirely by GDPR; EU states need not comply with Convention 108
B. Convention 108 provides a global floor for data protection; GDPR represents EU-specific higher standards applying in parallel
D. GDPR and Convention 108 are mutually exclusive legal regimes
C. Convention 108 applies only to non-EU countries; EU member states are exempt
questions.justification_correct: Convention 108 (modernized 2018) establishes global baseline; GDPR Article 94 recognizes it as complementary framework. EU states remain bound by both: GDPR for intra-EU, Convention 108 for third-country data transfers. EDPB Opinion 01/2021 confirms parallel applicability and convergence approach.
questions.justification_wrong: A overstates GDPR's exclusive reach; Convention 108 remains binding treaty. C incorrectly excludes EU states; they actively participate in Protocol modernization. D denies complementarity; Recitals 1, 163 explicitly reference Convention 108 alignment.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 6, a controller processes personal data of EU residents based on a 'legitimate interest' legal basis. The data subject challenges this basis as disproportionate. Which European institution has primary authority to review the balancing test and issue binding guidance on legitimate interest assessments across member states?
options: A. European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issuing Guidelines under Article 70(1)(e)
D. European Commission through delegated acts under Article 290 TFEU
C. Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) through preliminary rulings only
B. Individual Data Protection Authorities (DPAs) in each member state independently
questions.justification_correct: EDPB issues binding guidelines (Article 70(1)(e), GDPR) harmonizing Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interest interpretation across EU. These guidelines establish consistent standards for proportionality balancing, ensuring uniform enforcement.
questions.justification_wrong: While DPAs apply guidelines individually and CJEU interprets law, the EDPB's primary role is harmonizing standards through guidelines. Commission lacks direct binding authority over legitimate interest balancing tests.

questions.text: A data controller established in France processes health data (Article 9 GDPR) of patients across five EU member states. Under which circumstances can the controller rely on a single Article 27 representative designated in one member state for GDPR compliance across all five jurisdictions?
options: B. A single representative in any member state satisfies Article 27 requirements if the controller maintains a primary establishment in the EU
D. The representative requirement applies only to non-EU controllers; EU-established controllers need no representative
C. One representative in France (establishment location) satisfies Article 27 only if processing is limited to non-sensitive data
A. The representative must be designated in each member state where data subjects are located; one representative cannot serve all five
questions.justification_correct: Article 27(2) GDPR permits a single representative appointed in one EU member state for controllers established in another EU state. The representative acts for the controller in all member states, simplifying compliance across multiple jurisdictions.
questions.justification_wrong: Article 27 does not require multiple representatives per member state. Representatives are required for non-EU controllers. EU-established controllers may designate one representative satisfying EU-wide GDPR compliance.

questions.text: The ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) establishes security requirements for electronic communications via Article 4. How does this directive's scope relate to GDPR Article 32 security obligations, and which standard takes precedence in case of conflict?
options: D. The two instruments operate independently with no overlap; member states choose which to implement
B. Both instruments apply cumulatively; ePrivacy Directive applies to electronic communications, GDPR to all personal data; the higher standard prevails
C. ePrivacy Directive establishes minimum security for telecom networks; GDPR Article 32 applies only to data controllers' storage systems
A. ePrivacy Directive security rules are entirely superseded by GDPR Article 32; only GDPR applies
questions.justification_correct: ePrivacy Directive (Article 4) and GDPR Article 32 apply cumulatively within their respective scopes. ePrivacy covers electronic communications; GDPR covers all personal data processing. Where both apply, the higher/stricter standard governs (Recital 88 GDPR).
questions.justification_wrong: GDPR does not supersede ePrivacy Directive—both coexist. ePrivacy is not limited to telecom networks; it covers electronic communications broadly. Member states must implement both; they are not discretionary.

questions.text: Under Council of Europe Convention 108 (and its Protocol), a non-EU country accedes to the convention. What is the primary enforcement mechanism for data subject rights violations under Convention 108, and how does it differ from GDPR enforcement?
options: A. National courts and DPAs enforce Convention 108; the EDPB issues binding decisions on violations across signatory states
D. Individual data subjects may file complaints directly with the Council of Europe Secretary-General for immediate binding remedies
C. Convention 108 establishes an international court with binding jurisdiction over all signatory states, superseding national courts
B. State-to-state complaint mechanisms and national judicial remedies; no supranational enforcement body like the EDPB exists
questions.justification_correct: Convention 108 relies on state-to-state dispute resolution (Article 13) and national judicial/administrative remedies. Unlike GDPR's EDPB, no supranational enforcement body exists. Signatory states ensure domestic enforcement through national authorities.
questions.justification_wrong: EDPB only enforces GDPR within EU/EEA. Convention 108 has no international court or Secretary-General complaint mechanism. Data subjects rely on national courts and authorities within their respective jurisdictions.

questions.text: An online news publisher in Germany processes personal data of journalists and sources under Article 9(2)(e) GDPR (manifestly made public by data subject). The publisher receives a data subject access request (DSAR) from a journalist whose identity remains undisclosed in published articles. Under GDPR Articles 12-14, what is the publisher's obligation regarding identification of the data subject?
options: C. The publisher must request sufficient information to confirm the data subject's identity with reasonable certainty (Article 12(6)); if identification remains impossible, the DSAR may be refused
D. The publisher must provide all data in the dataset without verification, as Article 9(2)(e) exempts manifestly public data from identity verification
B. The publisher may refuse the DSAR entirely if the journalist cannot be identified, as identity verification is mandatory and non-negotiable
A. The publisher must honor the DSAR without identity verification if the journalist claims manifestly public status under Article 9(2)(e)
questions.justification_correct: Article 12(6) GDPR allows controllers to request additional information for identity verification if necessary to confirm the requester is the data subject. Refusal is permissible only if identification is impossible despite reasonable efforts. Article 9(2)(e) does not waive this requirement.
questions.justification_wrong: Article 9(2)(e) permits processing manifestly public data but does not exempt controllers from identity verification under Article 12(6). Manifestly public status does not eliminate DSAR verification obligations. Blanket refusal without reasonable identification efforts violates Article 12.

questions.text: A data controller established in France processes personal data of EU residents. Which supervisory authority has primary jurisdiction to oversee compliance with GDPR Article 55?
options: C. The EDPS, which has overarching authority over all GDPR enforcement
A. The French CNIL, as the controller's Member State authority
D. Each data subject's national DPA simultaneously with equal authority
B. The EDPB exclusively, as it supervises all EU processing
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 55 establishes that a controller established in a Member State is subject to that Member State's law. CNIL as France's DPA holds primary jurisdiction. Article 56 provides exceptions for specific cross-border cases requiring cooperation.
questions.justification_wrong: The EDPB is a coordination body, not a primary enforcement authority. EDPS supervises EU institutions. Multiple simultaneous authorities would create legal uncertainty contrary to Article 55's establishment principle.

questions.text: Under the ePrivacy Directive 2002/58/EC, a telecommunications provider wishes to process location data for direct marketing. Which lawful basis applies?
options: A. Legitimate interest of the provider, without prior consent
B. Prior explicit opt-in consent, except where technically impossible
D. Legal obligation derived from commercial registration
C. Soft opt-out consent, allowing marketing unless objected
questions.justification_correct: Article 13 ePrivacy Directive mandates prior explicit consent for location data processing in electronic communications. Recital 32 emphasizes opt-in as default. Marketing without consent violates this stricter standard than GDPR's legitimate interest.
questions.justification_wrong: Legitimate interest insufficient under ePrivacy Directive's prior consent mandate. Soft opt-out violates explicit consent requirement. Commercial registration creates no lawful basis for marketing communications under Article 13.

questions.text: A non-EU controller processes data of EU residents via a processor located in Germany. Under GDPR Article 3(2), which condition triggers EU law application?
options: D. Explicit contractual choice to apply GDPR voluntarily
B. Monitoring of behaviour of data subjects in the EU directed at them
C. The controller's headquarters location, regardless of processing location
A. The processor's location in Germany automatically applies GDPR
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 3(2)(b) applies EU law when processing monitors behaviour of individuals in the EU 'directed at them'. Recital 24 clarifies this targets profiling, personalised services, or online behaviour tracking regardless of controller location.
questions.justification_wrong: Processor location alone does not trigger scope. Controller location is insufficient under Article 3(2)'s extraterritorial reach. Voluntary choice applies Article 3(4), not the mandatory scope condition.

questions.text: The European Data Protection Board issues guidance on GDPR interpretation. What is the legal status of EDPB Guidelines for national DPAs?
options: B. Non-binding guidance to be considered in consistent application of GDPR
C. Purely advisory opinions with no relevance to enforcement decisions
D. Substitutes for national law that supersede DPA decisions
A. Binding regulations that DPAs must implement without discretion
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 70(1)(c) mandates EDPB issue guidelines for consistent application. Articles 70(2) & 74(2) establish non-binding consultation procedures. DPAs must consider Guidelines per Article 51(2) while retaining decision-making independence.
questions.justification_wrong: EDPB issues guidance, not binding regulations. Guidelines carry weight but allow DPA discretion. EDPB cannot override national law or substitute for DPA decisions per principle of supervisory independence (Article 52).

questions.text: A controller subject to GDPR transfers personal data to a processor in Switzerland. Which mechanism under Article 46 GDPR is most appropriate given the UK's adequacy decision precedent?
options: A. Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) combined with supplementary measures if necessary
B. Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) regardless of Switzerland's adequacy status
C. Reliance solely on Switzerland's previous CoE Convention 108 membership
D. No mechanism required, as Switzerland borders the EU
questions.justification_correct: Article 46(2) permits SCCs as appropriate safeguards. Schrems II (C-311/18) requires supplementary measures if SCCs alone insufficient. Switzerland lacks GDPR adequacy decision; SCCs with assessment of local laws provide compliant mechanism.
questions.justification_wrong: BCRs require multinational groups; SCCs suit processor relationships. CoE Convention 108 insufficient post-GDPR without Article 45 adequacy. Geographic proximity creates no GDPR exemption; transfers require Chapter 5 mechanism.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 6, a data controller processes personal data of EU citizens based on consent. The consent was obtained through pre-ticked boxes on a website. According to EDPB Guidelines 05/2020, what is the legal status of this consent mechanism?
options: C. Valid if the privacy notice is written in plain language
D. Valid under the legitimate interest exception if the data subject can easily withdraw consent
A. Invalid; GDPR requires explicit, freely given, specific, and informed consent; pre-ticked boxes do not satisfy 'freely given' requirement
B. Valid if the controller documents the consent timestamp and IP address
questions.justification_correct: EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 explicitly state pre-ticked boxes violate Article 7(4) GDPR. Consent must be freely given; silence or inactivity cannot constitute consent. Article 4(11) requires affirmative action.
questions.justification_wrong: Timestamps/documentation and plain language improve transparency but cannot cure the fundamental defect that pre-ticked boxes fail the 'freely given' requirement. Legitimate interest is separate from consent.

questions.text: A multinational enterprise has subsidiaries in Germany, France, and Poland. Each subsidiary appointed a local Data Protection Officer (DPO). The parent company in Luxembourg processes data centrally. Under GDPR Article 55, which DPA has primary supervisory authority?
options: C. The EDPB, as a multinational group requires centralized supervision
D. Collectively, all four DPAs must coordinate under the one-stop-shop mechanism, regardless of establishment
A. The DPA of each country where a subsidiary operates, as each subsidiary is an independent controller
B. The Luxembourg DPA, as the establishment of the main controller is in Luxembourg per Article 55(1)
questions.justification_correct: Article 55(1) GDPR establishes jurisdiction based on the controller's establishment. The parent company's Luxembourg establishment is the 'main establishment' for group processing, making Luxembourg DPA the lead supervisory authority under the one-stop-shop principle.
questions.justification_wrong: Subsidiaries may trigger local DPA interest, but Article 55 directs primary authority to the main establishment. EDPB supervises DPA cooperation, not direct processing. One-stop-shop follows Article 56 rules for main establishment.

questions.text: Under the ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) and its amendments, a telecommunications operator stores metadata (call records, location data) for 12 months for billing purposes. A government agency requests this data for criminal investigations without a warrant. What is the legal position?
options: A. The operator must comply under Article 15 ePrivacy Directive; law enforcement access overrides privacy protections
C. The operator may provide data if the government agency certifies a national security interest
D. The data retention itself is prohibited under GDPR Article 5(1)(e) as excessive
B. The request violates CJEU Tele2/Watson principles; law enforcement access requires judicial authorization absent an emergency
questions.justification_correct: CJEU Tele2 Sverige/Watson (C-203/15) held that general/indiscriminate metadata retention is disproportionate. Access by law enforcement requires prior judicial authorization under Articles 8 ECHR and 15(1) ePrivacy Directive.
questions.justification_wrong: Article 15 requires proportional restrictions; blanket access without warrant violates Case C-203/15. Security certifications lack legal basis. Retention for billing is legitimate; access restrictions remain independent of lawful retention.

questions.text: A data processor incorporated in Singapore processes personal data of UK residents on behalf of a US-established controller. Post-Brexit, the UK is deemed adequately protected under UK GDPR. The processor is NOT adequately protected. Which transfer mechanism is prohibited?
options: D. Transfer to Singapore under Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) if the processor is part of the controller's group
A. Transfer to Singapore under Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) if supplementary measures address the adequacy gap
B. Transfer to Singapore under adequacy decision, as Singapore is not recognized as adequate by UK ICO
C. Transfer to the US controller under adequacy for the US-EU/UK framework as both are deemed adequate
questions.justification_correct: The US is NOT currently deemed adequate under either GDPR or UK GDPR. Schrems II (C-311/18) and subsequent adequacy withdrawals require Chapter V mechanisms (SCCs, BCRs, adequacy). A blanket 'adequacy' transfer to the US is prohibited.
questions.justification_wrong: SCCs + supplementary measures, adequacy denials, and BCRs are all lawful mechanisms post-Schrems II. The US lacks adequacy; transfers require Chapter V safeguards, not presumed adequacy.

questions.text: The EDPB issues a binding decision under Article 65 GDPR resolving a dispute between the German DPA and Polish DPA regarding cross-border processing. A DPA subsequently issues a decision contradicting the EDPB ruling. Under Article 66 GDPR, what must occur?
questions.tag: EDPB Authority & Article 66 GDPR Dispute Resolution
options: C. The EDPB may impose a fine on the DPA for non-compliance under Article 84 GDPR
D. The decision is automatically void; the DPA must conduct a new investigation de novo
A. The dissenting DPA may issue its decision independently; Article 66 is advisory only
B. The dissenting DPA must withdraw or amend its decision to align with the EDPB ruling within 30 days per Article 66(4)
questions.justification_correct: Article 66(4) GDPR mandates that DPAs must follow EDPB binding decisions. A DPA must revise or repeal any conflicting decision within 30 days. This ensures legal consistency across EU data protection enforcement.
questions.justification_wrong: EDPB decisions are binding, not advisory (Article 65). GDPR lacks express fine mechanisms for DPA non-compliance. The conflicting decision is not automatically void but must be remedied by the DPA.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 4(11), what constitutes a 'relevant and reasonable expectation' for determining whether processing is transparent to a data subject?
options: D. Compliance measured solely by EDPB guidelines without national court interpretation
B. The subjective belief of the individual data subject regardless of presentation
C. Technical compliance with privacy notices exceeding 500 words
A. The perspective of a reasonable data subject considering the controller's presentation and context
questions.justification_correct: Article 4(11) and Recital 39 establish transparency through a reasonable person standard. EDPB Guidelines 4/2019 clarify expectations based on controller's presentation, prior relationships, and context. This objective approach ensures consistent application across jurisdictions.
questions.justification_wrong: Subjectivity lacks uniform enforcement; mere length doesn't guarantee transparency; EDPB guidance informs but doesn't replace national courts' interpretation of proportionality and fairness under Article 5(1)(a).

questions.text: Which provision establishes the European Data Protection Supervisor's (EDPS) power to audit EU institutions and bodies, and what is the scope limitation?
options: C. Article 28 GDPR; applies to all processors regardless of sector
D. Council Regulation 1049/2001; restricted to transparency requests
B. Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, Article 61; excludes CFSP/defence activities
A. Article 52 GDPR; limited to public sector only
questions.justification_correct: Regulation 2018/1725 (EU institutions' GDPR equivalent) Article 61 grants EDPS audit powers over EU bodies. However, common foreign and security policy (CFSP) and defence fall outside Article 3(2) scope per Article 2(2), reflecting Member State sovereignty.
questions.justification_wrong: GDPR Article 52 addresses national DPA cooperation, not EDPS; Article 28 concerns processor contracts; Regulation 1049/2001 governs document access, not audit authority.

questions.text: Under Council of Europe Convention 108+, which substantive right distinguishes it from GDPR regarding automated decision-making?
options: D. GDPR Article 22 is more restrictive than Convention 108+ regarding legal effect decisions
C. Convention 108+ requires human review; GDPR permits pure automated profiling with consent only
B. Convention 108+ Article 9 grants explicit right to explanation, while GDPR Article 22 permits exceptions for automated decisions
A. Convention 108+ provides identical automated decision protections without exception for legal obligations
questions.justification_correct: Convention 108+ Article 9 mandates explanation rights for significant automated decisions without broad exceptions. GDPR Article 22 permits exceptions for legal/contractual necessity (Article 22(2)), reflecting policy divergence. Convention 108+ stronger protections suit broader signatory base.
questions.justification_wrong: A overstates Convention 108+ similarities; C mischaracterizes GDPR consent provisions; D reverses restrictiveness hierarchy—GDPR permits more derogations than Convention 108+.

questions.text: The ePrivacy Directive 2002/58/EC Article 15(1) permits Member State derogations from confidentiality of communications. Which condition is NOT required for lawful derogation?
options: B. Derogation requires prior impact assessment published in Member State official journal
A. Derogation must be necessary in a democratic society for specific listed grounds (national security, public safety)
C. Derogation must be proportionate and limited in scope and duration
D. Derogation requires legislative basis, not executive decree alone
questions.justification_correct: Article 15(1) requires necessity, proportionality, and specificity for democratic societies. However, no explicit requirement mandates EDPB/DPA notification or official journal publication. Member States retain discretion over implementation transparency, though best practice requires it.
questions.justification_wrong: A, C, D all derive from Article 15(1) jurisprudence (S&M v. Austria, Tele2/Watson). Legislative basis requirement enforced by CJEU to prevent arbitrary executive surveillance authorization.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 6(1), which lawful basis does NOT require explicit or prior documentation in the privacy notice under Article 14?
options: C. Legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c)); references sufficient if obligation codified in EU/national law
B. Legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f)); requires balancing test and safeguards disclosure
A. Consent (Article 6(1)(a)); must include method and granularity details
D. Contractual necessity (Article 6(1)(b)); categories sufficient without itemizing each processing activity
questions.justification_correct: Article 14(2)(c) permits referencing legal obligation provisions without full recitation if obligation is publicly codified. Transparency achieved through legislative reference. EDPB Guidelines 4/2019 confirms references satisfy Article 14 minimalism for statutory bases.
questions.justification_wrong: A, B require substantive disclosure: consent method; legitimate interests balancing. D requires specificity—contractual necessity documentation must identify processing necessity, not merely claim contract existence per Article 14(2)(a).

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 4(11), which element is NOT required to establish valid consent as a lawful basis?
options: D. Granular consent distinguishable from other matters
B. Specific information about processing purposes before consent
A. Freely given affirmative action with clear indication of wishes
C. Prior notification to the supervisory authority of consent mechanisms
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 4(11) defines valid consent without mandating prior DPA notification. Recital 32 requires freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous consent; Articles 7 & 13-14 detail information requirements, but supervisory authority pre-notification is not a consent validity element.
questions.justification_wrong: Candidates may confuse GDPR transparency obligations (Article 13-14) or accountability principles (Article 5(2)) with consent validity. Article 36 requires DPA consultation only for high-risk processing, not routine consent mechanisms.

questions.text: Regarding the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) established under GDPR Article 68, which statement accurately reflects its institutional role?
options: B. EDPB issues binding decisions on individual complaints against data controllers in first instance
C. EDPB adopts Guidelines and consistency decisions that promote uniform GDPR interpretation across EU
D. EDPB directly investigates and enforces GDPR violations without member state DPA involvement
A. EDPB comprises heads of DPAs and is chaired by the European Commission representative
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 68(2) establishes EDPB composed of DPA heads. Article 70 authorizes Guidelines; Article 65 provides consistency decisions. EDPB ensures harmonized GDPR application. Articles 55-56 confirm DPAs retain primary enforcement jurisdiction over individual cases.
questions.justification_wrong: Articles 68-69 clarify EDPB is independent body chaired by DPA elected member, not Commission. Article 65 consistency decisions address DPA disagreements, not individual complaints. Enforcement remains DPA responsibility under Articles 55-56.

questions.text: Under the ePrivacy Directive 2002/58/EC (as amended by 2009/136/EC), which processing activity requires explicit consent before implementation?
options: B. Placement of non-essential tracking cookies that access terminal equipment without prior consent
C. Processing of publicly available website analytics aggregated at network level
A. Storage of cookies for essential session functionality on user device
D. Retention of communication metadata for legitimate network optimization purposes
questions.justification_correct: Directive 2002/58/EC Article 5(3) (amended by 2009/136/EC) mandates explicit prior consent for terminal equipment storage except strictly necessary cookies. Non-essential tracking cookies require affirmative consent. EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 clarify cookie wall practices violate consent requirements.
questions.justification_wrong: Essential cookies (session, security) are exempt under Recital 25 and Article 5(3). Aggregated analytics may qualify as legitimate interest. Metadata retention falls under eCommerce/telecom regulations with different consent thresholds than terminal equipment access.

questions.text: In applying GDPR Article 6(1)(f) (legitimate interest basis), how do recitals 47-49 characterize the requisite balancing test?
options: B. Three-part test: purpose necessity, legitimate expectation of processing, and reasonable balancing against fundamental rights
D. Legitimate interest applies only to non-personal data categories; personal data requires explicit consent always
C. DPA pre-approval is mandatory before any legitimate interest claim; absence voids lawfulness retroactively
A. Controller's economic benefit automatically outweighs data subject rights if processing is minimally invasive
questions.justification_correct: Recitals 47-49 establish three-part legitimate interest test: necessity for purposes, reasonable expectation balancing, and fundamental rights consideration. EDPB 06/2014 Opinion affirms balancing assessment. Article 6(1)(f) applies to personal data legitimately; no blanket consent requirement.
questions.justification_wrong: Economic benefit alone insufficient (Recital 48). DPA consultation (Article 36) applies only high-risk processing. Legitimate interest applies to personal data processing; Recital 47 confirms applicability across data categories with proportionality assessment.

questions.text: Under GDPR Article 49 (transfers to third countries), which mechanism allows adequacy decisions to remain valid despite subsequent national law changes in the recipient country?
options: C. Data subjects may unilaterally challenge transfers if third-country privacy laws subsequently weaken; Commission has no override
A. Adequacy decisions are self-executing; third-country legislative amendments automatically void transfers without Commission action
D. Adequacy decisions terminate automatically every three years unless both parties renew binding mutual agreements in writing
B. Commission adequacy decisions remain valid until Commission formally revokes them under Article 49(3) review procedures
questions.justification_correct: GDPR Article 45(3) empowers Commission to adopt adequacy decisions; Article 45(5) establishes review mechanisms. Commission may revoke under Article 45(4) if third country no longer ensures adequate protection. Decisions remain valid until formal revocation; Schrems II judgment (C-311/18) confirms ongoing monitoring obligation.
questions.justification_wrong: Adequacy decisions are Commission executive acts, not self-terminating on third-country law change. Article 49(1)(j) allows Standard Contractual Clauses as alternative; decisions don't auto-terminate triennially. Data subjects challenge enforcement through DPAs (Article 77), not direct transfer cessation.
